section.title: David Spares Saul at En-gedi
section.sectionRationale: Saul turns aside into the very cave where David and his men are hiding, and for one breathless moment David's men urge him to seize the chance to kill his enemy. Instead David only cuts the corner of Saul's robe, and even that stings his conscience. He confronts Saul with the proof, refuses to lift his hand against the LORD's anointed, and hands the whole matter over to the LORD as judge. It is one movement: the anointed-but-waiting king refusing to seize the throne by violence.
section.thesis: With Saul utterly in his power, David will not stretch out his hand against the LORD's anointed; he entrusts his cause and his vindication to the LORD who judges rightly, showing that the throne must be received from God's hand, not seized by the sword.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the trap in the cave, verses 1 to 4; David's conscience stricken, verses 5 to 7; David's appeal to the king, verses 8 to 15; and Saul's weeping response and oath, verses 16 to 22.

verses.text: After Saul had returned from pursuing the Philistines, he was told, "David is in the wilderness of En-gedi."
So Saul took three thousand chosen men from all Israel and went to look for David and his men in the region of the Rocks of the Wild Goats.
Soon Saul came to the sheepfolds along the road, where there was a cave, and he went in to relieve himself. And David and his men were hiding in the recesses of the cave.

So David's men said to him, "This is the day about which the LORD said to you, 'Behold, I will deliver your enemy into your hand, that you may do with him as you wish.'" Then David crept up and stealthily cut off a corner of Saul's robe.
Afterward, David's conscience was stricken because he had cut off the corner of Saul's robe.
So he said to his men, "The LORD forbid that I should do such a thing to my master, the LORD's anointed. May I never lift my hand against him, since he is the {{mashiach|LORD's anointed}}."
With these words David restrained his men, and he did not let them rise up against Saul. Then Saul left the cave and went on his way.

units.label: David's appeal to the king
units.note: may the LORD judge between us

verses.text: After that, David got up, went out of the cave, and called out to Saul, "My lord the king!" When Saul looked behind him, David bowed facedown in reverence
and said to Saul, "Why do you listen to the words of men who say, 'Look, David intends to harm you'?

Behold, this day you have seen with your own eyes that the LORD delivered you into my hand in the cave. I was told to kill you, but I spared you and said, 'I will not lift my hand against my lord, since he is the {{mashiach|LORD's anointed}}.'

See, my father, look at the corner of your robe in my hand. For I cut it off, but I did not kill you. Know and see that there is no evil or rebellion in my hands. I have not sinned against you, even though you are hunting me down to take my life.
May the LORD {{shaphat|judge}} between you and me, and may the LORD take vengeance on you, but my hand will never be against you.
As the old proverb says, 'Wickedness proceeds from the wicked.' But my hand will never be against you.
Against whom has the king of Israel come out? Whom are you pursuing? A dead dog? A flea?
May the LORD be our {{shaphat|judge}} and decide between you and me. May He take notice and plead my case and deliver me from your hand."

units.label: Saul weeps and swears
units.note: now I know you will be king

verses.text: When David had finished saying these things, Saul called back, "Is that your voice, David my son?" Then Saul wept aloud
and said to David, "You are more righteous than I, for you have rewarded me with good, though I have rewarded you with evil.
And you have declared this day how you have treated me well, for when the LORD delivered me into your hand, you did not kill me.
When a man finds his enemy, does he let him go away unharmed? May the LORD reward you with good for what you have done for me this day.
Now I know for sure that you will be king and that the kingdom of Israel will be established in your hands.
So now, swear to me by the LORD that you will not cut off my descendants or wipe out my name from my father's house."
So David gave his oath to Saul. Then Saul returned home, but David and his men went up to the stronghold.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one. Saul was set apart for the throne by anointing oil, and David refuses to touch him precisely because he bears this office, verses 6 and 10. The title binds kingship to the LORD's own choosing rather than to the sword, and later Scripture applies the same word to the ultimate Anointed One, Messiah, Christ.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, decide a case, vindicate. David will not act as his own avenger; twice, verses 12 and 15, he hands his cause to the LORD as the true Judge who will decide between him and Saul and clear his name in His own time.

basic.context: Saul is hunting David with three thousand men when he ducks alone into the very cave where David and his men are hiding. David's men see it as a gift from God, an open door to end the chase for good. Instead David only creeps up and cuts off a piece of Saul's robe, and even that small act troubles his conscience. He steps out, shows Saul the cloth as proof he could have killed him, and lets Saul know he answers to the LORD alone. Saul weeps, admits David is more righteous than he is, and confesses that David will indeed be the next king.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history, not a manual for handling every personal enemy, yet it teaches an enduring principle: David will not seize by violence what God has promised to give him in God's own time. The key question the chapter presses is how the anointed-but-waiting king should act toward the king he will replace, and the answer is restraint, respect for God's appointed office, and trust that God Himself will judge and vindicate.
basic.originalLanguages: David's men frame Saul's arrival as an opportunity, but David keeps calling Saul the LORD's mashiach, anointed one, twice refusing to lift his hand against him for that reason alone. Instead of taking justice into his own hands he twice appeals to the LORD as shaphat, judge, asking God to decide the case and grant vengeance in His own way and time.
basic.typology: David, already anointed king yet refusing to grasp the throne by force while the reigning king still lives, foreshadows Christ, the true Anointed One who would not seize an earthly kingdom by violence but received all authority from the Father in God's appointed way, after suffering and vindication.
basic.christ: David is God's anointed, promised the throne yet unwilling to take it by killing Saul. This restraint anticipates Christ, who though truly King refused the crowds who wanted to force Him onto a throne by might, and who entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly rather than defending Himself by force. Both wait for the Father to establish the kingdom in His time and way.
basic.god: The LORD is shown as the true Judge who sees hidden hearts and vindicates the innocent without needing human violence to do it. He has already anointed David and will keep His promise, but He does not ask David to fulfill it by sin; instead He calls David to wait, trust, and leave vengeance to Him.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 26 repeats this same test with Saul asleep in David's power. Romans 12 tells believers never to avenge themselves but to leave room for God's wrath. 1 Peter 2 says Christ entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly. Psalm 7, likely from this very period, calls on God as righteous judge.
basic.oneStory: This is one more step in the long road from David's private anointing to his public enthronement, showing that God's king is made by God's timing and God's hand, not by human violence. It points ahead to the true Son of David, who receives His kingdom the same way, through patient trust in the Father rather than seizing power by force.

context.literary: A tightly built scene: an ironic reversal where the hunter is exposed and helpless before the hunted, a private test of conscience, a public speech of self-defense and appeal to God, and a king's tearful confession. The repeated phrase the LORD's anointed frames the whole episode and supplies its moral center.
context.historical: David has already been secretly anointed by Samuel, 1 Samuel 16, but Saul still occupies the throne and hunts David as a rival. Ancient Near Eastern kingship regarded the anointed king as sacrosanct; killing him, even in self-defense, would have been seen as a grave and dangerous precedent for how David himself might one day be treated.
context.canonical: This scene, repeated with variation in chapter 26, establishes a pattern for how God's anointed servant relates to human authority even when that authority is unjust and murderous: patient trust rather than rebellion or usurpation, a pattern later urged on believers toward governing authorities and fulfilled perfectly in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that David, already anointed king, refused to seize the throne by killing Saul, entrusting his cause instead to the LORD as judge, and so to establish David's throne as a gift received from God rather than a prize taken by violence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes David's specific choice in a specific crisis; it does not prescribe cave etiquette or forbid all self-defense in every circumstance. What endures is the principle of leaving vengeance and vindication to God rather than seizing what God has promised by our own violent hand.

debates.question: Was cutting the corner of Saul's robe itself sinful, or only a symbolic diminishing of the king?

views.label: A minor but real trespass against the king's honor
views.case: David's own conscience convicts him immediately, verse 5, suggesting that even this small, non-lethal act was treated as an offense against the sanctity of Saul's anointed office.

views.label: A restrained, almost harmless proof, and the conscience is unusually tender
views.case: Cutting cloth caused no injury and was arguably the least violent way to prove Saul's life had been in David's hand; David's grief may reflect an unusually sensitive conscience rather than genuine wrongdoing.

debates.bearing: Either way the episode teaches the same lesson: even the smallest disrespect toward God's appointed authority troubled David, throwing into sharp relief his refusal to do Saul greater harm.

study.originalLanguages: Twice David calls Saul the LORD's mashiach, anointed one, verses 6 and 10, refusing to lift his hand against him purely because of that office, regardless of Saul's own wickedness. And twice, verses 12 and 15, David appeals to the LORD as shaphat, judge, asking God Himself to decide the case, take vengeance, and plead his cause, rather than doing so himself. The vocabulary shows a man who separates personal vindication entirely from personal violence.

typology.type: David, anointed king yet refusing to seize the throne by killing the reigning king
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Anointed One, who would not grasp an earthly kingdom by force but received all authority from the Father in His appointed way, Philippians 2:5-11 and John 6:15.

typology.type: David entrusting his cause to the LORD as judge rather than avenging himself
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who when reviled did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

study.christ: David is already God's anointed, yet he will not take by violence what God has promised to give him; he appeals instead to the LORD as the righteous Judge and waits for his vindication. This is a clear foreshadowing of Jesus, the true Messiah, who refused the temptation to seize an earthly throne by force, John 6:15, and who, unjustly hunted and condemned, did not retaliate but entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23. Both David and Christ receive the kingdom as a gift from God's hand in God's time, not as a prize wrenched away by the sword.
study.god: God is shown as the true Judge who sees every hidden motive, who has already determined to give David the kingdom, and who does not need David's sin to accomplish His promise. He calls His anointed to costly restraint, trusting that the Judge of all the earth will act justly and in His own time, rather than taking matters into human hands.

crossReferences.note: David again has Saul at his mercy, sleeping in the camp, and again refuses to touch the LORD's anointed, the same lesson repeated.
Never take revenge, but leave room for God's wrath, echoing David's refusal to avenge himself on Saul.
Christ entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly rather than retaliating when reviled and suffering.
Traditionally linked to David's flight from Saul, a prayer that calls on God as righteous judge over a false accuser.

study.oneStory: God's anointed king refuses to grasp the throne by violence, instead entrusting his cause to the LORD who judges rightly and will establish his kingdom in His own time. This is one more step toward David's throne, and it points ahead to great David's greater Son, who likewise refused to seize an earthly crown by force, trusting the Father's justice all the way to the cross and the resurrection that vindicated Him.

quiz.q: Why did David refuse to kill Saul in the cave even though his men urged him to?

options.text: Because Saul was the LORD's anointed, and David would not lift his hand against him.
options.note: Verses 6 and 10 give this as David's explicit reason, twice repeated.

options.text: Because he was afraid Saul's army would retaliate.
options.note: Saul's army was real danger, but the text gives David's reason as reverence for the anointed office, not fear of reprisal.

options.text: Because he had not yet decided whether Saul deserved death.
options.note: David's conviction is settled and immediate, not a matter of weighing Saul's guilt.

options.text: Because he lacked the opportunity to strike.
options.note: He had every opportunity; his men urged him to take it, and he chose not to.

quiz.q: Why was David's conscience stricken even over cutting Saul's robe?

options.text: Because even that small act showed disrespect toward the LORD's anointed king.
options.note: Verse 5 links his guilt directly to what he had done to Saul's robe, the anointed king's garment.

options.text: Because he feared Saul had seen him do it.
options.note: Saul did not notice at the time; David's grief is about the act itself, not being caught.

options.text: Because he regretted not killing Saul when he could.
options.note: The opposite is true; his conscience troubled him over doing even this much, not over sparing Saul.

options.text: Because his men mocked him for showing restraint.
options.note: The text records no mockery from his men; the conviction is entirely internal.

quiz.q: What did David ask the LORD to do in verses 12 and 15?

options.text: To judge between him and Saul, and to vindicate him rather than David avenging himself.
options.note: David twice hands the case to the LORD as judge instead of settling it by his own hand.

options.text: To strike Saul down immediately for his sin.
options.note: David asks God to judge and even take vengeance, but the focus is God's timing and justice, not an immediate demand for Saul's death.

options.text: To convince Saul's advisors that David was innocent.
options.note: David appeals to the LORD as judge, not merely to human opinion or advisors.

options.text: To give David the throne that very day.
options.note: David asks for vindication and deliverance, not for the throne to be seized immediately.

quiz.q: How did Saul respond to David's appeal?

options.text: He wept, confessed David was more righteous than he, and admitted David would be king.
options.note: Verses 16-20 record Saul's tears, his confession, and his acknowledgment of David's coming reign.

options.text: He apologized but continued hunting David the very next day.
options.note: Saul does return to pursuing David later in the story, but in this scene his response is genuine weeping and confession, not immediate renewed hostility.

options.text: He accused David of staging the whole encounter.
options.note: Saul does not accuse David of deception; he accepts the proof and weeps.

options.text: He ordered his men to seize David at once.
options.note: Saul does the opposite, extracting an oath from David and returning home.

quiz.q: How does David's restraint in this chapter point toward Christ?

options.text: David, already anointed, waits for God to give him the throne rather than seizing it by violence, as Christ refused to grasp an earthly kingdom by force.
options.note: Both David and Christ receive the kingdom as the Father's gift in His timing, not as a prize taken by the sword, John 6:15.

options.text: David proves that violence is always the fastest path to God's promises.
options.note: The chapter teaches the very opposite: David refuses violence and waits on God.

options.text: David shows that anointing guarantees immediate possession of the throne.
options.note: David was anointed long before this and still had to wait years for the throne, trusting God's timing.

options.text: David's mercy here has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: 1 Peter 2:23 draws exactly this connection, of entrusting oneself to the Judge who judges justly.

questions: David's men called Saul's arrival a gift from God to be seized; David saw it differently. Where are you tempted to call a shortcut to what you want God's providence, when it is really a temptation to sin?
Even cutting the robe stung David's conscience. How sensitive is your conscience to small compromises toward people you feel justified in resenting?

David refused to touch Saul because he was the LORD's anointed, regardless of Saul's own sin. What does it look like to respect the offices and positions God has placed over you, even when the person in them fails badly?
Twice David hands his case to the LORD as judge rather than settling it himself. Where do you need to release a grievance to God's justice instead of pursuing your own vindication?
Saul wept and confessed David was more righteous, yet later returned to hostility. How do you guard against a fleeting moment of conviction fading back into old patterns?

translationNotes.note: Went in to relieve himself renders a Hebrew euphemism literally meaning to cover his feet, noted in the BSB footnote; it explains why Saul was alone and vulnerable in the cave.
My father is a term of respect and appeal, not a literal claim of kinship, underscoring David's continued loyalty to Saul as his king even while proving his innocence.
A dead dog, a flea pictures something utterly insignificant and harmless, David's vivid way of asking why the king of Israel is expending such effort chasing so small a threat.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 24:1-22. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none appear anywhere in this file.
